perturbation.original_text: 4. The receiving Party hereby covenants that, during the validity period of this Agreement and for a period of five (5) years after its end or its termination, the Proprietary Information received from the disclosing Party shall: (a) be protected and kept in strict confidence by the receiving Party which must use the same degree of precaution and safegu ards as it uses to protect its own proprietary information of like importance, but in no case any less than reasonable care, (b) be only disclosed to and used by those persons who have a need to know and solely for the purpose specified in this Agreement, within the receiving Party's organisation and to its professional advisors, provided that in this latter case the disclosing Party so notifies the other Party and obtains the authorization from the other Party to make such disclosure, such authorizat ionnot being unreasonably withheld, and provided that such advisor(s) agree(s) to be bound by the terms and conditions of this Agreement. (Buyer shall be entitled to freely disclose such information to its affiliated companies, to its co -contractors for a project and to its final customer). (c) not be used in whole or in part for any purpose other than the purpose of this Agreement as specified in the preamble and for the Project above without the prior written consent of the disclosing Party, (d) neither be disclosed nor caused to be disclosed whether directly or indirectly to any third Party or persons other than t hose mentioned in subparagraph b) or the Buyer Clients or its co -contractors for the Project, (e) neither be copied, nor otherwise reproduced nor duplicated in whole or in part where such copying, reproduction or duplic ation have not been specifically authorized in writing by the disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. The receiving Party hereby covenants that, during the validity period of this Agreement and for a period of one (1) year after its end or its termination, the Proprietary Information received from the disclosing Party shall: (a) be protected and kept in strict confidence by the receiving Party which must use the same degree of precaution and safegu ards as it uses to protect its own proprietary information of like importance, but in no case any less than reasonable care, (b) be only disclosed to and used by those persons who have a need to know and solely for the purpose specified in this Agreement, within the receiving Party's organisation and to its professional advisors, provided that in this latter case the disclosing Party so notifies the other Party and obtains the authorization from the other Party to make such disclosure, such authorizat ionnot being unreasonably withheld, and provided that such advisor(s) agree(s) to be bound by the terms and conditions of this Agreement. (Buyer shall be entitled to freely disclose such information to its affiliated companies, to its co -contractors for a project and to its final customer). (c) not be used in whole or in part for any purpose other than the purpose of this Agreement as specified in the preamble and for the Project above without the prior written consent of the disclosing Party, (d) neither be disclosed nor caused to be disclosed whether directly or indirectly to any third Party or persons other than t hose mentioned in subparagraph b) or the Buyer Clients or its co -contractors for the Project, (e) neither be copied, nor otherwise reproduced nor duplicated in whole or in part where such copying, reproduction or duplic ation have not been specifically authorized in writing by the disclosing Party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) which does not specify a time period but requires reasonable measures.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA protects trade secrets, and while it doesn't specify a minimum protection period, a one-year confidentiality period may be deemed unreasonable for certain types of trade secrets, potentially undermining the reasonable measures required to maintain secrecy. Therefore, this modified period may fail to meet legal standards if a longer period is necessary for reasonable protection.

(2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modification reduces the confidentiality period from five years to one year. The Law Explanation states that while the DTSA doesn't specify a minimum protection period, a one-year period 'may be deemed unreasonable for certain types of trade secrets,' potentially failing to meet legal standards. The Perturbation Explanation supports this, noting that a one-year period may be 'insufficient' under the DTSA. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 (18 USC 1836) focuses on civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, it implicitly supports the idea that trade secrets must be protected. A shorter, potentially unreasonable protection period directly undermines the protection afforded by trade secret laws, creating a contradiction. The change directly impacts the required 'reasonable measures' to maintain secrecy as described by the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: 12. This Contract shall be governed by Buyer country laws and its appropriate courts of Paris. It is expressly agreed that if any dispute should arise, the competent jurisdiction of Paris in France shall be the sole competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.changed_text: 12. This Contract shall be governed by Supplier country laws and its appropriate courts of YYY. It is expressly agreed that if any dispute should arise, the competent jurisdiction of YYY shall be the sole competent jurisdiction.
perturbation.explanation: The original contract specifies French law and jurisdiction. If the supplier is not based in France and the contract is performed primarily outside France, this clause could be challenged as a forum selection clause that violates public policy, especially if the supplier's country has mandatory laws governing the dispute.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Principles of comity and public policy in international contract law.
perturbation.law_citation: Société Nationale Industrielle Aérospatiale v. United States District Court, 482 U.S. 522 (1987)
perturbation.law_explanation: Switching to the supplier's country law and jurisdiction could contradict principles of comity if the contract's performance and the relationship between the parties is primarily connected to France. Courts may refuse to enforce a forum selection clause if it violates strong public policy of the jurisdiction where enforcement is sought.

perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces the original contract's governing law and jurisdiction (France) with that of the supplier's country (YYY). The Perturbation Explanation suggests this could violate public policy if the contract's performance is primarily connected to France. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that courts may refuse to enforce a forum selection clause if it violates the strong public policy of the jurisdiction where enforcement is sought. Scraped Law Snippet 2, which includes keywords like 'France,' 'Jurisdiction and venue,' and 'Foreign signatory,' suggests that the issue of jurisdiction and forum selection in international contracts involving France has been considered in legal contexts. Therefore, the modification introduces a contradiction because it potentially violates principles of comity and public policy related to forum selection.

perturbation.original_text: 9. This Agreement shall enter into force on the date of its signature and shall be valid for a term of 5(five) years.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. This Agreement shall enter into force on the date of its signature and shall be valid for a term of 20(twenty) years.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the term to 20 years could be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade in some jurisdictions if it unduly restricts competition or the supplier's ability to work with others, violating antitrust principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: While not directly violating the Sherman Act, a 20-year NDA could be challenged as an unreasonable restraint of trade if it prevents the supplier from using generally known information or skills gained during the relationship, thereby limiting competition. This would particularly be the case if the information covered is not genuinely confidential or trade secrets.

Every person who shall make any contract or engage in any combination or conspiracy hereby declared to be illegal shall be
perturbation.justification: The changed text extends the contract term from 5 to 20 years, potentially creating an unreasonable restraint of trade. The Perturbation Explanation states that this could violate antitrust principles, especially if it restricts the supplier's ability to work with others. The Law Explanation supports this by suggesting the extended term could be challenged if it prevents the supplier from using generally known information or skills. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing 15 USC 1, declares illegal 'every contract, combination... in restraint of trade.' While the snippet doesn't define 'restraint of trade,' the extended contract term could be interpreted as such, making the modified clause likely to face legal challenges and therefore creating a legal contradiction. This falls under the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' as the time-sensitive obligation (contract term) may violate federal law.

perturbation.original_text: 5. Any Proprietary Information and copies thereof disclosed by either Party to the other shall remain the property of the dis closing Party and shall be returned by the receiving Party immediately upon request. The above provisions shall not apply to the Proprietary Information disclosed by the Supplier to the Buyer and which fall und er clause 12.2 of the General Purchase Conditions.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Any Proprietary Information and copies thereof disclosed by either Party to the other shall become the property of the receiving Party. The above provisions shall not apply to the Proprietary Information disclosed by the Supplier to the Buyer and which fall und er clause 12.2 of the General Purchase Conditions.
perturbation.explanation: Transferring ownership of the supplier's proprietary information to the buyer could violate intellectual property laws, particularly if the information includes patents or copyrights. This could constitute an unlawful taking of property.
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S. Code § 261 states that patents have the attributes of personal property. Changing the contract to stipulate that the buyer owns the supplier's proprietary information, including patented inventions, is a violation of the patent holder's ownership rights unless there is explicit and valid transfer of ownership separate from this agreement, which is not specified.

perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts 35 U.S. Code § 261, which states that patents have the attributes of personal property. By stipulating that the buyer owns the supplier's proprietary information, including patented inventions, the changed text violates the patent holder's ownership rights. The original contract ensures the proprietary information remains the discloser's property, aligning with patent law, whereas the modified version transfers ownership, creating a legal contradiction. The scraped snippet directly supports this, stating "patents shall have the attributes of personal property," which the modified contract circumvents.

perturbation.original_text: 10. The execution, existence and performance of this Agreement shall be kept confidential by the Parties hereto and shall not be disclosed by either Party without the prior written consent of the other.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. The execution, existence and performance of this Agreement shall be kept confidential by the Parties hereto and shall not be disclosed by either Party, even if legally compelled, without the prior written consent of the other.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'even if legally compelled' is problematic as it attempts to override legal obligations to disclose information in response to a subpoena, court order, or other legal process.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure, Rule 45
perturbation.law_explanation: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 45 governs subpoenas. A clause preventing disclosure even when legally compelled is unenforceable as it conflicts with the court's authority to compel testimony and production of documents. Attempting to contractually override this rule is invalid.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 45. Subpoena | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 45. Subpoena (a) In General. (1) Form and Contents. (A) Requirements—In General. Every subpoena must: (i) state the court from which it issued; (ii) state the title of the action and its civil-action number; (iii) command each person to whom it is directed to do the following at a specified time and place: attend and testify; produce designated documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things in that person's possession, custody, or control; or permit the inspection of premises; and (iv) set out the text of Rule 45(d) and (e). (B) Command to Attend a Deposition—Notice of the Recording Method. A subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition must state the method for recording the testimony. (C) Combining or Separating a Command to Produce or to Permit Inspection; Specifying the Form for Electronically Stored Information. A command to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things or to permit the inspection of premises may be included in a subpoena commanding attendance at a deposition, hearing, or trial, or may be set out in a separate subpoena. A subpoena may specify the form or forms in which electronically stored information is to be produced. (D) Command to Produce; Included Obligations. A command in a subpoena to produce documents, electronically stored information, or tangible things requires the responding person to permit inspection, copying, testing, or sampling of the materials. (2) Issuing Court. A subpoena must issue from the court where the action is pending. (3) Issued by Whom. The clerk must issue a subpoena, signed but otherwise in blank, to a party who requests it. That party must complete it before service. An attorney also may issue and sign a subpoena if the attorney is authorized to practice in the issuing court. (4) Notice to Other Parties Before Service. If th
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. Your browser does not support Javascript.
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the legal system's power to compel disclosure. The original text allows for legally compelled disclosure, while the changed text attempts to prevent it, even when legally required. This undermines the court's authority, as described in the Law Explanation, violating Rule 45, which governs subpoenas and compels testimony and production of documents. Scraped Law Snippet 1, detailing Rule 45, emphasizes the court's authority to command individuals to 'attend and testify; produce designated documents,' which the modified contract attempts to circumvent, thus creating a legal contradiction.
